design_philosophy.core_loop: Fast-paced first-person combat: navigate grid-based maze levels, eliminate Nazi guards and enemies, manage ammo/health, reach elevator or defeat boss to progress
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (ammunition, health) versus enemy pressure; spatial navigation and combat execution under threat
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Allied spy escaping and sabotaging Nazi strongholds; visceral gunplay and tactical movement through hostile bunkers
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Violent, fast-paced action game that deliberately shifted from family-friendly design toward brutality; established FPS genre conventions (grid-based navigation, sprite enemies, weapon variety, secret areas). The SNES port reflects Nintendo's censorship constraints, fundamentally altering thematic presentation while preserving core mechanics.

difficulty.lives_system: Player begins each episode with 4 lives; losing all health costs one life and restarts current level with knife, pistol, and 8 bullets
difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoint at level start only; original DOS version allowed mid-level saves, but many ports including SNES restrict saving to between-level only
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited agency; no difficulty selection documented. SNES port features 30 levels total (down from larger DOS version), suggesting reduced challenge curve. Secret areas and point-based rewards provide optional challenge extensions.

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon acquisition through level exploration and enemy drops: knife → pistol → submachine gun → chain gun. Health and ammunition refills scattered throughout levels
progression.unlock_mechanism: Key collection unlocks locked doors; secret wall tiles activate hidden rooms. Episodes progress sequentially with boss encounters at episode conclusions
replayability_hooks: Point scoring system with completion metrics (kill all enemies, collect all treasure, find all secrets, speedrun par times)
Multiple difficulty approaches for same level (speed vs. completionism)
Hidden level select and music test accessible via debug menu (R button on startup)

world_building_techniques: Environmental theming (Nazi bunkers and buildings establish setting)
Cutscene mission briefings (US/Europe version); Japanese version replaces these with episode title splash screens
Enemy variety and placement reinforce military/bunker setting

Nazi iconography and architecture (heavily censored in SNES: swastikas removed, Hitler renamed Staatmeister and mustache shaved, German voice acting redubbed to English, dogs replaced with giant rats per Nintendo policy)
Episode titles suggest narrative progression: 'Escape from Castle Wolfenstein' → 'Operation: Eisenfaust' → 'Die, Führer, Die!'

boss_design.philosophy: Capstone encounter at episode conclusion; drawn from both Wolfenstein 3D and its sequel Spear of Destiny (SNES version uses bosses sourced from Spear of Destiny for reasons not documented)
notable_bosses: Death Knight (referred to in Japanese v5 cutscene, suggesting phase-based or multi-encounter design)
Ubermutant (third-person firing animation unused in SNES, suggesting late-stage design changes)

faction_indicators: Nazi military hierarchy (guards, soldiers, officers implied by enemy variety)
Allied spy protagonist (William 'B.J.' Blazkowicz) establishes resistance faction
Castle Wolfenstein as enemy stronghold and power center
danger_signals: Nazi bunker environments establish totalitarian threat
Enemy types (guards, soldiers, dogs/rats) indicate organized military presence
Boss encounters represent apex of enemy power structure
Weapon progression (knife to chain gun) signals escalating conflict intensity
environmental_storytelling: Grid-based maze architecture reflects bunker design and fortification strategy
Treasure placement and secret areas suggest hidden resistance assets or loot
Locked doors and keys create spatial hierarchy and control zones
Enemy patrol patterns and placement reinforce military compound atmosphere
Health and ammo scarcity suggests ongoing war of attrition
